(A) Tuesday 24 February
(B) Friday 20 February
(C) Saturday 21 February
(D) Monday 23 February

<final_answer>
(A)
</final_answer>

Today is February 21, 2026. The Trump administration recently fired James W Hundley from his position as interim US Attorney for the Eastern District of Virginia. Who had the president previously appointed to the acting attorney role just days after Erik Siebert resigned from the position?
(A) James W Hundley
(B) Letitia James
(C) Todd Blanche
(D) James Comey
(E) Lindsey Halligan
explanation: The article states: "Days after Siebert's exit, Trump appointed Lindsey Halligan, a White House aide and the president's former personal attorney, to the position."
model_output:
Based on recent reporting:

After Erik Siebert resigned in September 2025, President Trump appointed Mary “Maggie” Cleary as the acting U.S. Attorney for the Eastern District of Virginia just days after Siebert’s departure. ([politico.com](https://www.politico.com/news/2025/09/20/trump-new-virginia-us-attorney-letitia-james-00574349?utm_source=openai))

So the correct answer is:

<final_answer>  
(C) Todd Blanche — *Incorrect*  
(A) James W Hundley — *Incorrect*  
(B) Letitia James — *Incorrect*  
(D) James Comey — *Incorrect*  
(E) Lindsey Halligan — *Incorrect*

Actually, none of the options listed match — the correct person is Mary “Maggie” Cleary, who is not among the options.

Today is February 21, 2026. NASA administrator Jared Isaacman announced on Saturday that the highly anticipated Artemis II lunar mission will almost definitely be delayed. What specific technical issue was cited as the cause for this expected delay?
(A) An interruption in the flow of helium required for launch operations.
(B) Hydrogen leaks detected in the rocket's seals and filters.
(C) A failure to fuel the rocket with 730,000 gallons of propellant over 50 hours.
(D) An issue with the space capsule's life support systems.
(E) A delay in the delivery of the SpaceX Starship lander.
explanation: The article reports: "But overnight on Friday, engineers observed an interruption in the flow of helium required for launch operations. 'This will almost assuredly impact the March launch window,' NASA said in a statement..."
model_output:
The specific technical issue cited by NASA Administrator Jared Isaacman was an **interruption in the flow of helium** in the Space Launch System’s interim cryogenic propulsion stage. ([wired.com](https://www.wired.com/story/nasa-delays-artemis-ii-launch-again?utm_source=openai))

So the correct option is:

<final_answer>
(A)
</final_answer>

CITATIONS:
- “NASA’s delay stems from a helium flow failure in the Space Launch System’s interim cryogenic propulsion stage…” ([wired.com](https://www.wired.com/story/nasa-delays-artemis-ii-launch-again?utm_source=openai))  
- “Last evening, the team was unable to get helium flow through the vehicle… This will almost assuredly impact the March launch window…” ([gulfcoastnewsnow.com](https://www.gulfcoastnewsnow.com/article/artemis-ii-march-launch-window-out-consideration-helium-failure/70447074?utm_source=openai))

Today is February 21, 2026. As the US, Canada, and Mexico prepare for the upcoming USMCA trade deal review, US Trade Representative Jamieson Greer highlighted specific obstacles in negotiations with Canada. What specific action by Canada did Greer cite as a "big challenge" during an interview?
(A) Canada's refusal to lift the 35% tariff on American fentanyl and drug imports.
(B) The broadcasting of an anti-tariffs advert sponsored by the province of Ontario during the World Series.
(C) Canada's refusal to sell US wine and spirits on their shelves.
(D) The failure to integrate the North American economies under the 1990s free trade deal.
(E) The decision to prioritize trade ties with Mexico over the United States before the July 1 deadline.
explanation: The article quotes Jamieson Greer stating: "They continue to have certain barriers. They refuse to sell US wine and spirits on their shelves.,' he said. 'There are a variety of issues... and this makes it a big challenge and an obstacle for starting real negotiations with them.'"
model_output:
Greer specifically cited Canada’s refusal to sell U.S. wine and spirits on their shelves as a “big challenge” in the negotiations. So the correct answer is:

<final_answer>
(C)
</final_answer>

CITATIONS:
- “U.S. Trade Representative Jamieson Greer … said … ‘They continue to have certain barriers. They refuse to sell U.S. wine and spirits on their shelves …’ … this makes it a big challenge …” ([eenews.net](https://www.eenews.net/articles/us-trade-rep-says-canada-making-it-hard-to-begin-talks-on-usmca-review/?utm_source=openai))  
- “Canada’s most-populous province, Ontario, to ban U.S. wines and spirits from the shelves at Ontario-run liquor stores.” ([eenews.net](https://www.eenews.net/articles/us-trade-rep-says-canada-making-it-hard-to-begin-talks-on-usmca-review/?utm_source=openai))

===============================================

Today is February 21, 2026. NASA is aiming for an early March launch for its Artemis II mission after completing a successful "wet dress rehearsal". However, a previous rehearsal attempt in early February had to be aborted. What specifically caused the early termination of that first rehearsal?
(A) The crew was required to go into mandatory quarantine.
(B) An interruption in the flow of helium used to pressurize fuel tanks.
(C) Unfavorable weather conditions at the Kennedy Space Center.
(D) A hydrogen fuel leak at the launch pad.
(E) Issues with the Orion capsule's life support systems.
explanation: The text notes: "The first rehearsal, which took place at the beginning of February, ended early because of a hydrogen fuel leak at the launch pad."
model_output:
The correct answer is:

<final_answer>  
(D) A hydrogen fuel leak at the launch pad.  
</final_answer>

Reasoning:

- The first Artemis II wet dress rehearsal attempt in early February was **terminated at the T-5:15 minute mark** because of a **liquid hydrogen leak** at the *tail service mast umbilical interface*, where high concentrations of hydrogen were detected earlier in the countdown. ([nasa.gov](https://www.nasa.gov/blogs/missions/2026/02/03/artemis-ii-wet-dress-rehearsal-test-terminated-at-t-515/?utm_source=openai))  
- The leak was the cause—specifically a hydrogen leak—not any issue with helium flow (that came up later), nor life support systems, nor weather requiring quarantine. ([space.com](https://www.space.com/space-exploration/artemis/nasa-will-fuel-up-its-artemis-2-moon-rocket-for-the-2nd-time-on-feb-19-will-it-leak-again?utm_source=openai))  

Thus the early abortion was due to (D).

===============================================

Today is February 21, 2026. Writer and philanthropist Anna Murdoch-Mann has died at the age of 81. During her 31-year marriage to Rupert Murdoch, she authored several novels. What was the specific subject of her 1988 novel, 'Family Business'?
(A) A memoir about her 31-year marriage to Rupert Murdoch.
(B) A fictionalized account of the 1969 abduction of Muriel McKay.
(C) A historical account of her time as a reporter for the Sydney Daily Telegraph.
(D) A chronicle of the succession battles between her children Elisabeth, James, and Lachlan.
(E) A multi-generational media dynasty.
explanation: The article states: "She penned several novels during their marriage, including the 1988 Family Business about a multi-generational media dynasty."
model_output:
The correct answer is:

<final_answer>  
(E)  
</final_answer>  

Here is how this matches the details of *Family Business* by Anna Murdoch:

- The novel is a **fiction** tracing the rise and fall of a **media dynasty**, centered around Yarrow McLean, daughter of a newspaper family, who builds a global news empire. ([washingtonpost.com](https://www.washingtonpost.com/archive/lifestyle/1988/01/13/passion-the-publisher/7d9d2047-6205-43ef-a3ba-448eb5f80dab/?utm_source=openai))  
- Key themes include her taking over her father’s newspaper, dealing with rival business interests, expanding into bigger media ventures, and the consequences of ambition within the family. ([washingtonpost.com](https://www.washingtonpost.com/archive/lifestyle/1988/01/13/passion-the-publisher/7d9d2047-6205-43ef-a3ba-448eb5f80dab/?utm_source=openai))  

Thus, (E) “A multi-generational media dynasty” precisely describes the specific subject of the 1988 novel.  

Option breakdown for clarity:

- (A) is wrong — it isn’t a memoir.  
- (B) is wrong — the book is not about the abduction of Muriel McKay.  
- (C) is wrong — it’s not about her time as a reporter per se.  
- (D) is a subordinate theme but not the primary focus.  

CITATIONS:  
- Passion the Publisher review, The Washington Post. ([washingtonpost.com](https://www.washingtonpost.com/archive/lifestyle/1988/01/13/passion-the-publisher/7d9d2047-6205-43ef-a3ba-448eb5f80dab/?utm_source=openai))  
- Publishers Weekly summary. ([publishersweekly.com](https://www.publishersweekly.com/9780688068585?utm_source=openai))

Today is February 21, 2026. Actor Eric Dane has passed away 10 months after revealing his ALS diagnosis. Before his death, how did Dane specifically use his acting career to raise awareness about his condition?
(A) He appeared on an episode of 'Brilliant Minds' playing a firefighter struggling to accept an ALS diagnosis.
(B) He returned to the set of 'Euphoria' to film a final season centered around a character with motor-neurone disease.
(C) He starred as an on-screen surgeon with ALS in the medical drama 'Grey's Anatomy'.
(D) He produced and starred in a documentary video for Target ALS to raise $1bn in federal funding.
(E) He played a clinician in an Oxford University-sponsored film about the rapid progression of neurodegenerative brain disorders.

model_output:
Before his death, Eric Dane used his acting career to raise awareness about ALS in the following way:

He guest-starred on an episode of the medical drama *Brilliant Minds*, playing a firefighter with ALS who struggles to accept and reveal his diagnosis. ([yahoo.com](https://www.yahoo.com/entertainment/celebrity/articles/eric-dane-grateful-continue-acting-011025260.html?utm_source=openai))

This corresponds to:

<final_answer>  
(A)  
</final_answer>

Today is February 21, 2026. A woman was recently charged after abandoning her two-year-old dog at Las Vegas's Harry Reid International Airport. When questioned by police at the departure gate, what specific reason did she give for leaving the animal behind?
(A) She claimed she had arranged for the charity Retriever Rescue of Las Vegas to collect the animal.
(B) She stated the airline wouldn't let her fly with the dog and claimed it had a tracking device, implying it would return to her.
(C) She stated that she had lost the required documents for the golden doodle to fly as a service dog.
(D) She claimed the dog was aggressive and had to be left tied to a baggage sizer for passenger safety.
(E) She said she was fleeing a hostile situation and could not afford the fees to board the animal.
explanation: According to the police statement in the article: "When asked why she abandoned the dog, she stated the airline would not allow her to fly with it and claimed the dog had a tracking device - implying it was acceptable to leave the animal behind and it would return to her".
model_output:
When questioned by police at the departure gate, she said “the airline wouldn’t allow her to fly with it and claimed the dog had a tracking device — implying it would be acceptable to leave the animal behind and it would return to her.” ([people.com](https://people.com/woman-arrested-for-abandoning-dog-at-airport-when-after-service-dog-documentation-was-rejected-11909443?utm_source=openai))

So the correct answer is:

<final_answer>
(B)
</final_answer>

===============================================

Today is February 21, 2026. The Supreme Court has struck down President Trump's use of the IEEPA for global tariffs. According to analysis of the ruling's political impact, why might some of Trump's conservative allies in Congress be "breathing somewhat easier" following this decision?
(A) Because they now have explicit authorization to draft a new tariff bill before the midterm elections.
(B) Because the ruling guarantees that tariff revenue will remain in the US Treasury instead of being refunded.
(C) Because Trump's tariffs and their costs to consumers have been unpopular, making Republican candidates vulnerable to Democratic attacks.
(D) Because the decision forces America's trading partners to make concessions ahead of the State of the Union Address.
(E) Because it allows them to push forward with efforts to end birthright citizenship and reshape the federal government.
explanation: The article explains: "In fact, some of Trump's conservative allies in Congress may be breathing somewhat easier with this decision. The president's tariffs - and the costs they have imposed on consumers - have been unpopular among many Americans. Republican candidates in battleground states and congressional districts would have been open to Democratic attacks for supporting Trump's policies."
model_output:
Conservative allies in Congress might be “breathing somewhat easier” after the Supreme Court’s decision for the following reason:

- They have long feared that Trump’s sweeping tariffs—imposed under his use of emergency powers via IEEPA—were politically risky, because consumers and swing voters blame them for rising prices, cost-of-living pressures, and inflation. Polls and public opinion data show many Americans believe tariffs are one major cause of higher daily costs—food, electronics, etc.—which can hurt Republicans in the 2026 midterms.([cfr.org](https://www.cfr.org/articles/cfr-poll-shows-americans-across-party-lines-tie-tariffs-to-affordability?utm_source=openai))

By striking down many of Trump’s tariffs, the Court reduces those political liabilities: fewer policies are exposed to attack by Democrats as inflation-drivers, fewer congressional Republicans have to defend unpopular cost increases, especially in swing districts. Thus, the ruling gives them some relief from the pressure of defending the tariff regime. This dynamic most closely matches Option (C).

Here’s a quick review of why the other options are less likely:

- (A) There is *no* indication that the ruling gives Congress explicit authorization to draft a new tariff bill. The decision limits executive power under IEEPA; it does not transfer authority to legislative bodies.  
- (B) The Court’s opinion *raises questions* about refunds of the tariffs already collected—there’s concern that tariffs struck down could lead to reimbursement to importers. So it does *not* guarantee revenue will remain.([theguardian.com](https://www.theguardian.com/us-news/2026/feb/20/trump-supreme-court-tariffs-ruling?utm_source=openai))  
- (D) The ruling doesn’t directly force trading partners to make concessions before specific future events like a State of the Union Address. There’s no link in analyses to such a diplomatic timeline.  
- (E) Ending birthright citizenship and broad reshaping of government are unrelated to the Supreme Court’s decision on IEEPA—they concern wholly different constitutional and statutory issues.  

<final_answer>  
(C)  
</final_answer>

CITATIONS:  
- Council on Foreign Relations (CFR-Morning Consult poll on tariffs, cost-of-living) ([Link](https://www.cfr.org/articles/cfr-poll-shows-tariffs-affordability))  
- Guardian (Poll in swing House districts blaming tariffs for rising prices) ([Link](https://www.theguardian.com/us-news/2025/oct/23/voter-survey-trump-tariffs-inflation-healthcare))  
- Fortune (Goldman Sachs report: cost-of-living, political risks to GOP from tariff regime) ([Link](https://fortune.com/2025/12/18/why-is-economy-bad-tariffs-midterms-affordability-cost-of-living/))  
- Global Trade Alert (Analysis of tariff reductions and rule-changes after SCOTUS) ([Link](https://globaltradealert.org/reports/SCOTUS-IEEPA-Tariff-Impact))

===============================================

Today is February 21, 2026. The Supreme Court's decision to invalidate some of President Trump's emergency tariffs has shifted the global trade landscape. Despite the highly tariffed environment last year, what specific factor allowed China's trade to still flourish with the US?
(A) A massive pivot toward agricultural exports to the European Union and the UK.
(B) The collection of $240bn in tariff revenues directly from US-based manufacturers.
(C) American importers absorbing the costs of clothing and toys without raising prices.
(D) A surge in imports of IT hardware into the US amid the AI boom.
(E) Successful negotiations that exempted Chinese goods from the "Liberation Day" tariffs.
explanation: The article explicitly states: "But China has still flourished, thanks to a surge in imports of IT hardware into the US amid the AI boom."
model_output:
The correct answer is:

<final_answer>  
(C)  
</final_answer>

---

**Explanation:**

Despite sweeping tariffs, one major factor that allowed China’s trade—especially exports of consumer goods like clothes, toys, etc.—to still flourish with the U.S. is **importers in the U.S. absorbing much of the cost** rather than immediately passing it on to consumers.

Here’s how current reporting and research support this:

---

## Evidence supporting (C)

1. **Tariff burden falling on U.S. businesses and consumers**  
   - A New York Fed study found that importers (U.S. businesses) bore ~94% of the burden of U.S. tariffs from China in early 2025.([visaverge.com](https://www.visaverge.com/taxes/u-s-firms-bore-nearly-90-of-tariff-costs-new-york-fed-study-finds/?utm_source=openai))  
   - Another analysis showed that U.S. businesses absorbed about 64% of tariff costs by mid-2025, with consumers and foreign exporters bearing less.([tralac.org](https://www.tralac.org/blog/article/16868-who-actually-pays-the-tariff-it-s-complicated.html?utm_source=openai))  

2. **Businesses delaying price increases to maintain competitiveness**  
   - Surveys from the New York–Northern New Jersey region in mid-2025 revealed many firms facing high import costs chose to **absorb the tariffs** rather than raise prices immediately.([libertystreeteconomics.newyorkfed.org](https://libertystreeteconomics.newyorkfed.org/2025/06/are-businesses-absorbing-the-tariffs-or-passing-them-on-to-their-customers/?utm_source=openai))  
   - Firms in highly competitive sectors often kept retail prices stable for a time to avoid losing market share.([uschamber.com](https://www.uschamber.com/small-business/small-businesses-big-burden-the-cost-of-tariffs?utm_source=openai))  

3. **Impact on imports of low-margin goods**  
   - Sectors with thinner margins—like clothing and toys—were hit by high tariffs, but their imports didn’t collapse thanks to importers absorbing the extra costs, at least initially.([ndtvprofit.com](https://www.ndtvprofit.com/world/how-the-ai-hardware-boom-has-become-chinas-new-export-engine-10768590?utm_source=openai))  

---

## Why the other options don’t match as well

- **(A)** A pivot toward agricultural exports to the EU/UK – China’s export growth during the tariff regime was mostly driven by high-tech and electrical goods, not agriculture.([ndtvprofit.com](https://www.ndtvprofit.com/world/how-the-ai-hardware-boom-has-become-chinas-new-export-engine-10768590?utm_source=openai))  
- **(B)** $240 B in tariff revenues collected from U.S. manufacturers – While there was massive tariff revenue, the burden was not predominantly placed on manufacturers in isolation, and the figure is off or mis‐attributed.([cathaygeneralbancorp.com](https://www.cathaygeneralbancorp.com/about-us/insights-by-cathay/us-china-2025-annual-economic-report?utm_source=openai))  
- **(D)** Surge in imports of IT hardware amid the AI boom – This did happen (AI-related goods were up), but China’s trade with the U.S. in some tech sectors dropped sharply; much of the growth was via other markets or through third-party suppliers.([businessinsider.com](https://www.businessinsider.com/china-tech-exports-us-trump-tariffs-ai-global-demand-2025-9?utm_source=openai))  
- **(E)** Exemptions from "Liberation Day" tariffs – No credible evidence of sweeping exemptions for Chinese goods that would explain the scale of trade flourishing. Exceptions were small and isolated.([cathaygeneralbancorp.com](https://www.cathaygeneralbancorp.com/about-us/insights-by-cathay/us-china-2025-annual-economic-report?utm_source=openai))  

---

## Conclusion

China’s trade was able to “flourish”—or at least maintain strength—despite heavy U.S. tariffs largely **because American importers absorbed many of the costs** in order to avoid sudden price shocks to consumers, preserve market share, and maintain demand. Over time, costs may shift toward consumers, but the immediate effect was that importers — businesses in the U.S. — bore an outsized share of the tariff burden. That is precisely what option **(C)** describes.

Today is February 21, 2026. Following the Supreme Court's ruling against the IEEPA tariffs, President Trump has enacted new global tariffs using Section 122 of the Trade Act of 1974. Under these new rules, what is the specific trade status of textiles and apparel imported from Costa Rica and Honduras?
(A) They are subject to a 10% tariff because the de minimis exemption was ended last year.
(B) They are subject to a 15% tariff as part of the new global temporary tariff structure.
(C) They are exempt from all tariffs solely because of the USMCA agreement.
(D) They face reciprocal industry-specific tariffs under Section 232 of the Trade Expansion Act.
(E) They remain duty-free under the Dominican Republic-Central America Free Trade Agreement.
explanation: The article notes: "Similarly, textiles and apparel from Costa Rica, the Dominican Republic, El Salvador, Guatemala, Honduras, and Nicaragua will remain duty-free under the Dominican Republic-Central America Free Trade Agreement."
model_output:
Under the Section 122 proclamation (effective 24 February 2026 for 150 days, later raised to 15%) the temporary tariff duties do not apply to textiles and apparel imports from Costa Rica and Honduras *if* those goods are entering duty-free under CAFTA-DR. ([whitehouse.gov](https://www.whitehouse.gov/fact-sheets/2026/02/fact-sheet-president-donald-j-trump-imposes-a-temporary-import-duty-to-address-fundamental-international-payment-problems/?utm_source=openai))

Therefore the correct status is:

<final_answer>
(E)
</final_answer>

CITATIONS:
- White House Fact Sheet (section122 proclamation exempts CAFTA-DR textiles/apparel) ([whitehouse.gov](https://www.whitehouse.gov/fact-sheets/2026/02/fact-sheet-president-donald-j-trump-imposes-a-temporary-import-duty-to-address-fundamental-international-payment-problems/?utm_source=openai))  
- Global Trade Alert report (confirms CAFTA-DR textiles/apparel are exempt) ([globaltradealert.org](https://globaltradealert.org/reports/S122-US-Tariff-Estimates?utm_source=openai))

(A) He suffered a knee injury that forced him out of the semi-final.
(B) He engaged in a physical altercation within the first nine seconds of the match.
(C) He threw up while sat on the bench due to a sickness bug afflicting the squad.
(D) He collapsed on the ice after scoring a goal on the half-volley.
(E) He was forced off the ice after being cross-checked by a Swedish defenceman.
explanation: The article states: "The squad has also been afflicted by a sickness bug, which dramatically revealed itself in the quarter-final when Nathan MacKinnon threw up while sat on the bench."
model_output:

(C)
</final_answer>

The news about injured player referred to in the quarter-final was about *Sidney Crosby*, who suffered a lower-body/leg/knee injury against Czechia after hits from Radko Gudas and Martin Nečas. ([nhl.com](https://www.nhl.com/news/topic/olympics/sidney-crosby-injury-update-for-canada-against-czechia-in-olympic-quarterfinals?utm_source=openai))

Today is February 21, 2026. President Trump has stated he is considering a limited military strike on Iran to pressure its leaders over their nuclear program. According to analysis, what specific domestic political risk does a protracted, multi-week conflict with Iran pose for the Trump administration?
(A) It would lead to the immediate impeachment of the president due to narrow Republican majorities in Congress.
(B) It could provoke a direct military retaliation against the USS Gerald R Ford and USS Abraham Lincoln.
(C) It would derail ongoing negotiations with US Special Envoy Steve Witkoff in Geneva.
(D) It threatens to unite the liberal members of the Supreme Court against his executive powers.
(E) It threatens alienating parts of Trump's Maga base who supported his promise to end messy foreign entanglements.
explanation: The article states: "...a protracted, multi-week conflict with Iran threatens alienating parts of Trump's Maga base which were drawn to his campaign promise of an end to messy foreign entanglements."
model_output:
A protracted, multi-week conflict with Iran carries a serious domestic political risk for the Trump administration: it threatens to alienate parts of his MAGA base who supported his promise to end messy foreign entanglements. Option (E) captures that risk.

Here’s the reasoning, based on recent analysis:

---

### Why (E) is the correct one

- The MAGA base has long included a strong anti-war, “America First” strand, wary of U.S. involvement in wars overseas. Analysts note figures like Tucker Carlson, Steve Bannon, Charlie Kirk, and others warning that a drawn-out conflict with Iran would betray Trump’s campaign pledge to avoid “forever wars.” ([theguardian.com](https://www.theguardian.com/us-news/2025/jun/22/iran-attack-maga-reaction-trump?utm_source=openai))

- Polling and commentary indicate that while many MAGA voters have expressed support for strikes on Iran’s nuclear sites, there is also considerable unease among them about escalation, open-ended war, and what military entanglement would cost domestically. ([thedispatch.com](https://thedispatch.com/article/donald-trump-iran-maga-republicans/?utm_source=openai))

- Some conservative leaders are warning that pursuing regime change or escalating military operations risks breaking the trust of those in the base who primarily supported Trump because he promised not to get involved in messy, prolonged foreign wars. ([washingtonpost.com](https://www.washingtonpost.com/politics/2026/02/21/trump-us-iran-maga-base/?utm_source=openai))

---

### Why the other options are not supported by current analysis

- (A): There is no indication that any impeachment efforts are imminent or built around Republican opposition in Congress due to a conflict with Iran. That’s not seen as a realistic domestic political risk in the short term.

- (B): While there is risk of Iranian retaliation against U.S. assets, the question asks specifically about a political risk *at home*—this concerns internal political dynamics, not battlefield or military operational risks.

- (C): I found no credible reporting about “ongoing negotiations with U.S. Special Envoy Steve Witkoff in Geneva” or that military conflict would derail any specific named talks by that person. That seems invented.

- (D): The Supreme Court uniting its liberal wing against executive power over foreign policy isn’t part of the analysis currently—Congressional authorization is debated, but the Court has not emerged as a central axis of opposition in the present moment.

---

So, the major domestic risk is that a long conflict could alienate significant segments of Trump’s own political base—those who backed him because of promises to reduce foreign military commitments. That’s option (E).

<final_answer>
(E)
</final_answer>

CITATIONS:
- Washington Post – “Trump’s MAGA base raged against Iran strikes last year. This time, it’s quieter,” analysis of MAGA opposition to extended conflict. ([washingtonpost.com](https://www.washingtonpost.com/politics/2026/02/21/trump-us-iran-maga-base/?utm_source=openai))  
- The Guardian – “Trump’s saber-rattling over Iran threatens to split his Maga base,” discussing internal MAGA warnings and risks of foreign entanglements. ([theguardian.com](https://www.theguardian.com/us-news/2025/jun/18/trump-iran-israel-maga-policy?utm_source=openai))

Today is February 21, 2026. Former 'Grey's Anatomy' star Eric Dane has died from ALS, a disease for which there is currently no cure. According to Professor Kevin Talbot of the University of Oxford, what is the primary reason why a cure for ALS has not yet been found?
(A) The rapid progression of the disease makes it impossible to administer experimental clinical trial drugs.
(B) Federal funding for ALS research has been consistently blocked by government bureaucracy.
(C) Pharmaceutical companies prioritize treating more common diseases like Alzheimer's and cancer.
(D) The condition does not have a single unifying cause.
(E) Patients typically refuse to take part in the necessary clinical research studies.
explanation: The article details: "Medical treatments and technologies can improve the quality of life of those with ALS, but no cure has been found. Professor Kevin Talbot, who runs the University of Oxford's Nuffield Department of Clinic Neurosciences, told the BBC this is because it does not 'have a single unifying cause'."
model_output:
Based on Professor Kevin Talbot’s research and public commentary, the primary reason why a cure for ALS has not yet been found is that: the condition lacks a single unifying cause. ALS is biologically heterogeneous: there are many genetic, environmental, and cellular pathways implicated, which differ between individuals. ([pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/24010870/?utm_source=openai))

So the correct answer is:

<final_answer>
(D)
</final_answer>

Today is February 21, 2026. In addition to broad global levies, President Trump has made other recent changes to US trade rules. Last year, the president ended a specific policy known as the "de minimis exemption". What exactly did this exemption allow?
(A) An exemption that allowed goods from Canada and Mexico to avoid the fentanyl tariffs under the USMCA.
(B) A policy that permitted critical minerals and pharmaceuticals to be imported without reciprocal tariffs.
(C) A rule that shielded small businesses from pursuing costly refunds for unlawful IEEPA taxes.
